instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Gold Savings Scheme
Gold holds a special sentiment in Indian households. It’s not just a shiny metal, but security, tradition, and a reliable asset passed down through generations. From ancient times to the present, families have used gold to safeguard their wealth, combat inflation, and prepare for life’s significant moments.
With this deep cultural and financial connection, modern gold savings schemes have become a popular way to systematically purchase gold without straining your budget. In this article, we’ll cover what gold savings schemes are, why they matter, how you can invest, and the key factors to consider before putting your money into one.
What is the Gold Savings Scheme?
A gold savings scheme is a simple and structured way to purchase gold gradually, rather than paying a large amount at once. It works much like a monthly savings plan where you deposit a fixed sum each month, usually anywhere from ₹500 to ₹10,000. Instead of earning returns, you benefit through discounts on your final instalment or small interest payouts, depending on how the scheme is designed. Many jewellers and financial institutions offer their own versions of these schemes, each with small variations in benefits and terms.
Over the years, the government has also introduced gold-related programs to utilise the idle gold and bring more transparency to the market. This led to the rise of options such as the Gold Monetisation Scheme, Sovereign Gold Bonds, Gold Coin and Bullion Scheme, each serving a different purpose in helping people invest, save, or earn through gold.
Note: As per RBI’s notice, the Gold Monetisation Scheme is no longer available for mid-term and long-term deposits, effective March 26, 2025.
Types of Gold Savings Schemes Available in India
Some of the available gold savings schemes in India are explained below:
Jewellery-Based Gold Savings Schemes
Jewellery-based gold savings schemes are the most common option people choose, especially when planning a future purchase such as a necklace or bangles. These schemes let you deposit a fixed amount every month, and at the end of the tenure, you can use the accumulated value to buy gold. Instead of earning interest, you usually receive a discount on the last instalment, which increases your total purchasing power.
Let us understand with a simple example:
Imagine joining a 12-month scheme with a monthly instalment of ₹3,000. The jeweller offers an 85% discount on the final instalment.
Monthly payment: ₹3,000
Total deposited over 12 months: 11 instalments x ₹3,000 = ₹33,000
Discount benefit: 85% of the last instalment, so you pay only 15% (₹450)
Total value you can shop for: ₹33,000 + ₹450 = ₹33,450
So even though you paid ₹33,450 from your pocket, you get to buy jewellery worth ₹36,000.
Most schemes allow early withdrawal, but the discount tends to reduce, so you may not get the full benefit if you exit before the lock-in period ends.
Bank-Offered Gold Savings Schemes
Bank-backed gold schemes operate very differently from jewellery schemes. Here’s how they typically work in simple terms:
First, your gold is checked for purity by an authorised centre.
After you give permission, the gold is melted and refined.
You’re then issued a certificate of deposit, which becomes the basis of your gold savings account with the bank.
The refined gold is stored securely, and its value is locked at a pre-decided rate set by the bank and refinery.
When it’s time to redeem your deposit:
Short-term deposits can be withdrawn in either cash or gold.
Long-term deposits can only be taken back in cash.
Features and Benefits of Gold Saving Schemes
Here are the features of investing in a gold savings scheme in a bank:
You can deposit gold coins, bars, or jewellery (after removing stones) and start earning interest on it, just like a bank deposit.
The minimum deposit is 10 grams, with no upper limit, making it suitable for both small and large investors.
Gold deposited is recorded up to three decimal points, ensuring precise valuation.
You can open a Gold Deposit Account with designated banks after completing basic KYC formalities.
Deposits are made through authorised collection and testing centres, where purity is checked in your presence before issuing a receipt.
Once confirmed, the bank credits your Gold Deposit Account (for short-term) either on the same day or within 30 days of deposit, whichever comes first.
Interest begins accruing from the date your gold is converted into tradable bars, or 30 days after deposit, whichever happens sooner.
Some of the benefits of investing in a gold savings scheme in India are covered below:
Protects Against Inflation:
Gold tends to hold or increase its value even when prices rise, making it a strong inflation shield.
Diversifies Your Portfolio:
Since gold moves differently from stocks and bonds, adding it reduces overall investment risk.
Highly Liquid:
Gold can be quickly converted into cash, offering a reliable backup during emergencies.
Safe Haven Asset:
In uncertain economic or political times, gold is seen as a stable and trustworthy investment.
Tangible Wealth:
Physical gold offers comfort, security, and can be easily passed down to future generations.
Tax Efficiency:
In schemes like GMS, you don’t pay capital gains tax on the profits earned, and both the interest and the maturity amount are exempt from income tax, making it a highly efficient way to grow idle gold.
Why Gold Savings Schemes Are Important in India?
Gold savings schemes have become one of the most popular investment plans in India due to the following reasons:
Cultural and Traditional Significance
Gold has always been more than an investment in India, woven into rituals, celebrations, and weddings. Since many households plan to buy gold gradually, gold savings schemes help people accumulate it comfortably over time, rather than making a big one-time purchase.
Hedge Against Inflation
Gold is widely regarded as a safe store of value, especially during uncertain times. Its ability to stay liquid and usually rise faster than inflation makes it a reliable way to protect your wealth and maintain purchasing power.
Financial Planning
With gold savings schemes, buying gold is easier by allowing small monthly deposits that add up over time. Benefits like reduced making charges or small bonuses further help you achieve your goals, whether it’s purchasing wedding jewellery or simply expanding your gold portfolio, without straining your monthly budget.
Eligibility Criteria for Investing in Gold Saving Schemes
To invest in most gold savings schemes in India, you need to fall under the categories defined by regulatory guidelines:
Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs)
Trusts, including those linked to mutual funds or ETFs registered under SEBI
Individuals applying singly or jointly
Companies
Registered charitable organisations
Proprietorship firms
Partnership firms
Central or state government bodies and government-owned organisations
Factors to Consider Before Investing in a Gold Saving Scheme
Here are some factors that you must consider before investing in any gold savings scheme:
Provider Reputation and Trustworthiness
Before signing up for any gold savings scheme, take time to check the jeweller’s credibility and the bank’s reputation. Not every provider follows the same standards, so it’s important to review their track record, customer feedback, and transparency before committing your money.
Gold Price Volatility Risk
Gold prices fluctuate, which can impact your final purchase price. If the price falls during your savings period, you may end up paying more than what you would have paid if you bought gold outright at the lower market rate.
Charges, Tenure and Tax Implications
Before picking a gold savings scheme, know how much you can contribute monthly and how long you want to stay invested. Compare interest or discounts: big names may offer lower perks, while smaller providers might provide better value. Also, consider penalties for early withdrawal if flexibility is required. It’s wise to review the tax side to see whether the scheme brings benefits or liabilities.
Key Takeaway
Gold has been a trusted asset in India for centuries, and gold savings schemes make it easier to buy it gradually.
Gold savings schemes let you invest small monthly amounts and receive benefits like discounts or interest instead of direct profits.
Two main types exist: jeweller-based schemes that offer discounted instalments, and bank-backed schemes that work through certified deposits and gold accounts.
These schemes offer features like interest (under GMS), high liquidity, diversification, inflation protection, and tax advantages.
They matter because they align with tradition, protect against inflation, and help with planned gold purchases.
While the eligibility criteria are broad, choosing the right provider and understanding risks, charges, tenure, and taxes is crucial before you invest.
Conclusion
Gold savings schemes provide a straightforward way to blend your love for gold with smart financial planning. Whether you're preparing for a wedding, marking a festival, or just building long-term wealth, these plans make owning gold easier and more manageable. Once you understand how the schemes work and the factors to consider, you can select an option that truly aligns with your goals and helps you increase the value of your investment.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
What forms of gold are allowed under gold saving schemes?
You can deposit different forms of gold depending on the scheme. Some plans only accept monthly cash contributions to purchase gold, while bank-linked schemes may accept gold coins, bars, or plain jewellery without stones.
How is the value of gold calculated in a gold savings scheme?
The value is usually based on the day’s gold rate set by the jeweller or the bank at the time of purchase or redemption.
Is there a minimum or maximum investment limit in gold schemes?
Jeweller schemes usually start with small monthly deposits. Bank schemes may require a minimum quantity of gold and have no upper limit.
Are gold savings schemes safe or risky for investors?
They are generally safe, but risks exist mainly in gold price fluctuations and the credibility of the jeweller. Bank-backed schemes carry regulatory oversight and are considered more secure.
Is there any government-backed gold savings scheme in India?
Yes. The Gold Monetisation Scheme (GMS) and Sovereign Gold Bond (SGB) are government-supported options that let investors earn interest on gold holdings or digital gold.
Can I exit or break the lock-in period of a gold savings scheme?
Jeweller schemes may allow early exit, but with penalties or reduced benefits. Bank-linked GMS has strict lock-in rules depending on tenure, and premature withdrawal is permitted only under specific conditions.
Disclaimer
The content provided is for general informational purposes only. The material is compiled from publicly available sources, internal insights, and other information deemed reliable. While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.

same day or within 30 days of deposit, whichever is earlier. 7. Interest begins accruing from the date your gold is converted into tradable bars, or 30 days

When gold prices rise with time, your gold investments can grow and create a corpus for your financial goals. 2. Diversifies Your Portfolio:

Once you understand how the schemes work and the factors to consider, you can select an option that truly aligns with your goals and helps you increase the value of your investment.
